title: The Philip Seymour Hoffman Hall of Fame
format: Ten years after his death, Sean and Amanda walk Philip Seymour Hoffman's entire filmography — from a 1991 golf micro-movie to his last le Carré spy — then argue it down to a Hall of Fame of ten.

segments.heading: The Philip Seymour Hoffman Hall of Fame

films.title: Boogie Nights
films.note: Scotty J, the boom operator — a sensitive, funny, devastatingly sad portrait of a man who just wants to be accepted. Sean saw it in a theater where a man stood up and clapped when Scotty first appeared. Green on sight.

films.title: The Talented Mr. Ripley
films.note: Freddie Miles, bounding out of the convertible — 'don't you want to fuck every woman you see just once?' Takes the movie from a cast of stars; the Arrow screening had the whole room erupt.

films.title: Almost Famous
films.note: Lester Bangs, the calm guardian angel of the film. Every line quotable; the mainstream-movie/awards fusion the hosts 'all saw at Christmas together.'

films.title: Along Came Polly
films.note: Sandy Lyle — 'make it rain,' then 'raindrop!' clanking off the backboard from 38 feet. A real physical-comedy performance, and the male version of the Judy Greer best-friend archetype.

films.title: Capote
films.note: Truman Capote — the full transformation in voice, posture and weight that won Best Actor. In for the win, even though Sean stays mixed on the film itself.

films.title: Before the Devil Knows You're Dead
films.note: Andy, half of a pair of brothers robbing their parents' jewelry store — a desperate man who only unravels further. One of Sidney Lumet's last films; a credible romance with Marisa Tomei.

films.title: Charlie Wilson's War
films.note: Gust Avrakotos — the electric office scene with John Slattery ('I learned Finnish!'). The YouTube-generation clip that carries an otherwise mediocre film; led to Willy Loman on Broadway under Mike Nichols.

films.title: Synecdoche, New York
films.note: Caden Cotard — the internal, restrained, quiet-and-sad counterweight to Charlie Wilson. A twin-killer with it for Sean and Amanda; a movie its lovers love forever.

films.title: Moneyball
films.note: Art Howe — does with stillness what another actor would need scenes for, the disgust visible behind the desk at Brad Pitt. A Bennett Miller reunion.

films.title: The Master
films.note: Lancaster Dodd — automatic green. The wedding speech, the processing scenes, the final song; the fullest showcase of everything he did, and both hosts' likely No. 1.

films.title: Triple Bogey on a Par Five Hole
films.note: One of a few hard-to-see micro-movies at the very start, before he was really himself on screen.

films.title: Zooler
films.note: Another obscure early credit; title taken from the on-air mention (verify).

films.title: My New Gun
films.note: Small, hard-to-find early film.

films.title: Leap of Faith
films.note: His first big Hollywood movie — a roadie for Steve Martin's traveling televangelist. Very small part.

films.title: Scent of a Woman
films.note: The sniveling, privileged George Willis Jr. opposite Chris O'Donnell and Pacino — a breakthrough small part. Yellowed for the turning point, then cut as sentimental.

films.title: My Boyfriend's Back
films.note: A minor studio comedy from a big movie-going year for Sean.

films.title: Joey Breaker
films.note: Minor early credit.

films.title: Money for Nothing
films.note: A John Cusack thriller-comedy; all pretty minor.

films.title: The Getaway
films.note: The Alec Baldwin/Kim Basinger remake — a heist guy who gets shot and kicked out of a car by Michael Madsen. Not in.

films.title: When a Man Loves a Woman
films.note: A skeleton-key three-scene turn as a rehab patient bonded to Meg Ryan's character; the role casting directors noticed. Not in.

films.title: Nobody's Fool
films.note: A very small part in Robert Benton's Paul Newman film.

films.title: Hard Eight
films.note: PTA's first feature — the Young Craps Player who mercilessly mocks Philip Baker Hall across the table. Honorary yellow, then cut.

films.title: Twister
films.note: Dusty Davis — the part he took to bank enough money to move back to New York. Fun, but not in.

films.title: Montana
films.note: A star-studded thriller (Kyra Sedgwick, Stanley Tucci, Robin Tunney); not a great movie, not in.

films.title: The Big Lebowski
films.note: Brant, the Coens' obsequious assistant — the office-tour scene Sean calls the funniest of the 1990s. Yellowed, then cut, fondly.

films.title: Next Stop Wonderland
films.note: A mansplaining ex-boyfriend named Sean, later glimpsed protesting on TV. Not in.

films.title: Happiness
films.note: Todd Solondz's discomforting film, lost to time and physical media — a very notable, very uncomfortable role. Not in.

films.title: Patch Adams
films.note: Amanda's teenage favorite, which the hosts agree absolutely stinks and is even a little dangerous. Not in.

films.title: Flawless
films.note: A Joel Schumacher drama — a drag queen next door to De Niro's stroke-stricken cop. A good performance in a movie that hasn't aged well. Not in.

films.title: Magnolia
films.note: Phil Parma, the nurse tending Jason Robards — his most open-hearted character, entrusted with holding a whole section of the film together. Yellowed, then cut once Boogie Nights and The Master covered the PTA slot.

films.title: State and Main
films.note: A tortured writer in David Mamet's lightly satirical romantic comedy opposite Sarah Jessica Parker. Good movie, not in.

films.title: Love Liza
films.note: A widower huffing gasoline through his grief, in a film by his friend Todd Louiso. Punishingly sad; another gym-basketball scene. Not in.

films.title: Punch-Drunk Love
films.note: Dean Trumbell, the mattress-store sex-line operator — the phone-call explosion that changes the movie's register. Not quite in, but a great scene.

films.title: Red Dragon
films.note: Freddy Lounds, a disgusting tabloid man disfigured by Lecter, under Brett Ratner. Not in.

films.title: 25th Hour
films.note: Jacob Elinsky, the private-school teacher and trust-fund fourth wheel to Edward Norton and Barry Pepper. One of Sean's 25 favorite films; he finds the pathos, but it's not a Hall of Fame lead. Not in.

films.title: Owning Mahowny
films.note: Another very good indie in his one-for-them-one-for-me stretch. Not in.

films.title: The Party's Over
films.note: An earnest documentary he hosted and helped make about the 2000 election — a bizarre time capsule where you see the real Hoffman for a long stretch. Not in.

films.title: Cold Mountain
films.note: A preacher in Anthony Minghella's celebrated adaptation — a Ripley reunion with Jude Law. Not in.

films.title: Strangers with Candy
films.note: A very small part in the comedy. Not in.

films.title: Mission: Impossible III
films.note: Owen Davian — the sinister whisper and 'I'm going to find her and hurt her.' The whole defense of the film; possibly his most widely seen movie. Yellowed, then cut.

films.title: The Savages
films.note: A sad sibling drama with Laura Linney (a banned Christmas-Day pick). Recommended, but a red.

films.title: Doubt
films.note: Father Brendan Flynn, sparring with Meryl Streep in some of the best-acted scenes of the decade — but neither host loves the script. Out.

films.title: The Invention of Lying
films.note: Just a cameo; cut from consideration.

films.title: Mary and Max
films.note: The titular Max — his only animated feature, a sad, very good film. Not in.

films.title: The Boat That Rocked
films.note: The Count, the pirate-radio DJ, in one of the lesser Richard Curtis movies. Fun, not in.

films.title: Jack Goes Boating
films.note: His directorial debut — worth seeing for John Ortiz, his real-life close friend. Not in.

films.title: The Ides of March
films.note: A James Carville-esque campaign manager dueling Paul Giamatti in Clooney's flawed political drama. One five-minute scene of pure Hoffman; can't in good faith go in.

films.title: A Late Quartet
films.note: An unshowy turn in an indie about classical musicians; feels like he's there so the movie can get made. Notable, not in.

films.title: The Hunger Games: Catching Fire
films.note: Plutarch Heavensbee, the gamemaker — he brings the gravitas the franchise needs. Fine, not in.

films.title: God's Pocket
films.note: A grimy Philly crime saga, John Slattery's directorial debut and a Charlie Wilson reunion. Not in.

films.title: A Most Wanted Man
films.note: A dissolute, resigned German intelligence officer in the le Carré adaptation — his final completed lead, with a small, devastating breakdown. Green through the walk-through, then cut in the final round.

films.title: The Hunger Games: Mockingjay - Part 1
films.note: Technically his last film, though he didn't complete his work on it. Not in.
